title: The organizational mission is understood and informs cybersecurity risk management
description: The organization's mission, objectives, stakeholders, and activities are understood and used to inform cybersecurity risk decisions, including AI system deployments.
ai_notes: AI systems must be evaluated against organizational mission — autonomous AI decisions must align with organizational values and risk appetite.

title: Internal and external stakeholders are understood, and their needs inform cybersecurity risk management
description: Stakeholders with interest in AI systems — including regulators, customers, and employees — are identified and their cybersecurity needs addressed.
ai_notes: AI system stakeholders include data subjects, model consumers, regulators (EU AI Act, CCPA), and internal teams.

title: Legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements regarding cybersecurity are understood and managed
description: Legal and regulatory obligations relevant to AI systems are identified and compliance is maintained.
ai_notes: Includes EU AI Act risk classification, CCPA/GDPR for training data, and sector-specific AI regulations (healthcare, finance).

title: Risk management objectives are established and agreed to by organizational stakeholders
description: AI risk tolerance and risk appetite are defined, documented, and agreed upon by leadership.
ai_notes: AI risk tolerance must address model failure modes, bias, hallucination rates, and adversarial robustness thresholds.

title: Risk appetite and risk tolerance statements are established, communicated, and maintained
description: Documented risk appetite statements cover AI-specific risks including model drift, data poisoning, and inference attacks.
ai_notes: Risk tolerance for AI must specify acceptable false positive/negative rates, adversarial robustness thresholds, and drift limits.

title: A standardized method for calculating, documenting, categorizing, and prioritizing cybersecurity risks is established and communicated
description: AI risk scoring methodology is standardized and applied consistently across all AI systems.
ai_notes: Integrates with Meridian risk scoring — asset risk scores from the knowledge graph feed this subcategory's evidence.

title: Policy for managing cybersecurity risks is established based on organizational context, cybersecurity strategy, and priorities
description: AI security policy is documented and covers the full ML lifecycle: data acquisition, training, deployment, monitoring, and retirement.
ai_notes: Policy must address agentic AI behaviors, autonomous decision-making boundaries, and human oversight requirements.

title: Organizational leadership is responsible and accountable for cybersecurity risk and fosters a culture that is risk-aware
description: Executive accountability for AI security risk is established, with clear ownership of AI system risk decisions.
ai_notes: AI governance requires designated roles: AI Risk Owner, Model Risk Officer, and AI Ethics Board representation.

title: Inventories of hardware managed by the organization are maintained
description: Physical and virtual infrastructure supporting AI workloads is inventoried — GPUs, TPUs, inference servers, and training clusters.
ai_notes: AI compute infrastructure (GPU clusters, inference endpoints) must be included in hardware asset inventory.

title: Inventories of software, services, and systems managed by the organization are maintained
description: AI models, inference APIs, training pipelines, model registries, and ML frameworks are inventoried as software assets.
ai_notes: Integrates with Meridian asset inventory — AI asset types include AIModel, InferenceAPI, TrainingData, MLPipeline, ModelRegistry.

title: Inventories of data and corresponding metadata for designated protection are maintained
description: Training datasets, validation sets, model weights, and inference logs are inventoried with sensitivity classifications.
ai_notes: Training data provenance, PII classification, and data lineage must be tracked. Includes synthetic data and third-party datasets.

title: Vulnerabilities in assets are identified, validated, and recorded
description: AI-specific vulnerabilities — model weaknesses, adversarial susceptibility, supply chain risks — are identified and tracked.
ai_notes: Includes ML framework CVEs, prompt injection vulnerabilities, and model extraction risks. Integrates with Meridian vulnerability scoring.

title: Cyber threat intelligence is received from information sharing forums and sources
description: Threat intelligence on AI-specific attack campaigns is consumed from MITRE ATLAS, MITRE ATT&CK, and sector ISACs.
ai_notes: MITRE ATLAS provides AI-specific threat actor TTPs. Meridian knowledge graph provides cross-referenced threat intelligence.

title: Threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts are used to understand inherent risk and inform risk response prioritization
description: AI risk assessments combine threat actor intelligence, asset criticality, vulnerability severity, and attack path analysis.
ai_notes: Meridian risk scores (0-10) provide quantitative inherent risk inputs. Parallel failure mode scoring models multi-path attack risk.

category: Identity Management, Authentication, and Access Control
title: Identities and credentials for authorized users, services, and hardware are managed by the organization
description: AI service accounts, API keys, model access tokens, and inference endpoint credentials are managed under identity governance.
ai_notes: Inference APIs require strong authentication. Model registry access must be role-restricted. API keys for LLM services must be rotated.

category: Identity Management, Authentication, and Access Control
title: Access permissions, entitlements, and authorizations are defined in a policy, managed, enforced, and reviewed
description: Least-privilege access to AI assets — training data, model weights, inference APIs — is enforced and regularly reviewed.
ai_notes: Access to high-criticality assets (TrainingData containing PII, AIModel weights) must follow need-to-know. Rate limiting on InferenceAPIs mitigates model extraction.

title: The confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-at-rest are protected
description: Training data, model weights, and inference logs stored at rest are encrypted and integrity-verified.
ai_notes: Model weights are high-value IP. Training data containing PII requires encryption at rest. Integrity verification prevents data poisoning at storage layer.

title: The confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-in-transit are protected
description: Inference requests, model outputs, and training data transfers are encrypted in transit.
ai_notes: Inference API traffic must be TLS-encrypted. Prompt content and model responses may contain sensitive data requiring transport-level protection.

title: Configuration management practices are established and applied
description: AI infrastructure configurations — model serving parameters, API rate limits, container security — are managed as code and version-controlled.
ai_notes: Model serving configuration (batch size, timeout, rate limits) directly affects adversarial robustness and availability.

title: Networks and environments are protected from unauthorized logical access and usage
description: AI inference endpoints and training infrastructure are network-segmented and protected from unauthorized access.
ai_notes: Inference APIs should be behind API gateways with authentication. Training clusters should be network-isolated. Model registries require access logging.
